3A_Bibliographic.citation: Nagata, J. M., Singh, G., Yang, J. H., Smith, N., Kiss, O., Ganson, K. T., Testa, A., Jackson, D. B., & Baker, F. C. (2023). Bedtime screen use behaviors and sleep outcomes: Findings from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study. Sleep Health, 9(4), 497-502.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: 10,280 individuals had complete data available for inclusion in this analysis. The current study reports data from Year 2, when participants were predominantly 11-12 years old (2018-2020).

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Bedtime screen use behaviors (9 items: TV/movies, video games, music, phone/text, social media, chat rooms, browsing internet, computer for studying, reading) and device/phone behaviors
3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: Screen Use Around Bedtime questionnaire (adapted from Lemola et al. 2014 and Arora et al. 2014) plus National Sleep Foundation poll items
3C_Exposure.modalities: TV/movies, video games, music, phone/text, social media, chat rooms, browsing internet, computer for studying, reading; also device in bedroom (yes/no) and phone behavior at bedtime
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous (Likert 1-5 frequency scale for bedtime activities); binary/categorical for device in bedroom and phone behavior
3C_Exposure.time_frame: Past week (bedtime activities); total recreational screen time as typical weekday/weekend
3C_Exposure.reporter: Self-report (youth) for bedtime screen use and KSADS; caregiver-report for Sleep Disturbance Scale
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: A 9-item measure was administered to adolescents to assess engagement in screen activities in bed before sleeping. On a 5-point Likert scale ranging from 1 (never) to 5 (every night), adolescents reported how often (in the past week) they engaged in the following activities while already in bed before going to sleep: watch TV or movies, play video games, listen to music, talk on the phone or text, spend time online on social media, participate in chat rooms, browse the Internet, use a computer/laptop for studying, and reading.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Trouble falling or staying asleep (youth-reported) and overall sleep disturbance (caregiver-reported)
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: KSADS DSM-5 (single item, binarized) and Sleep Disturbance Scale for Children (SDSC, cutoff >= 39)
3D_Outcome.base_rate_if_binary: 15.5% trouble falling/staying asleep; 28.0% overall sleep disturbance
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Adolescents were asked one question about having problems falling or staying asleep in the past 2 weeks when tired and wanting to sleep, adapted from the KSADS DSM-5. Responses were binarized into 2 categories. Caregivers completed a 26-item measure (Sleep Disturbance Scale for Children). A cutoff of 39 was used to indicate the presence of sleep disturbance.
3D_Outcome.notes: This is the same data/sample as paper 033 (Helmer et al. 2024 in BMC Public Health). Nagata is senior author on both.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Modified Poisson regression with robust standard errors to estimate adjusted risk ratios; propensity weights applied
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parent education, study site, data collection period (pre- vs. during COVID-19 pandemic), depression
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Modified Poisson regression analyses using robust standard errors were conducted to calculate risk ratios estimating associations between screen time before bed and during the night (exposure variable) and sleep problems or disturbance (outcome variables), adjusting for confounders. Propensity weights were applied to yield representative estimates based on the American Community Survey. Statistical significance was evaluated after implementing the Benjamini-Hochberg Procedure to address a false discovery rate of 0.05.

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: TV or electronic device in bedroom
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Having a TV or electronic device in the bedroom was positively associated with self-reported trouble falling or staying asleep (adjusted risk ratio [ARR] 1.27, 95% CI 1.12-1.44)

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Spend time in chat rooms at bedtime
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Adjusted risk ratio per unit increase in frequency
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: 1.27 (95% CI 1.17-1.39) for spending time in chat rooms while already in bed before going to sleep

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Adjusted risk ratio per unit increase in frequency

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Spend time on social media at bedtime
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Adjusted risk ratio per unit increase in frequency
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Spend time online on social media: ARR 1.16 (1.10, 1.23), p<.001
Total Recreational Screen Time: ARR 1.04 (1.03, 1.05), p<.001

Being woken up by phone calls, text messages, or emails after trying to go to sleep on one or more nights in the past week was associated with a higher relative risk of trouble falling or staying asleep (ARR 1.28, 95% CI 1.19-1.39)

3G_Reporting_Quality.large_n_verbatim: The large, diverse, and population-based sample is a major strength
3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: Some effect sizes were small, particularly for the caregiver reports of sleep disturbance.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Health care providers could educate parents and adolescents on specific screen behaviors around bedtime. Family media use plans informed by The American Academy of Pediatrics may help facilitate conversations about household screen rules, such as limiting screen use in the hour before bedtime.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: which may be a simple behavioral modification for some adolescents to prevent sleep disturbance
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion focuses on the specificity of bedtime screen behaviors (as opposed to total screen time) in their associations with sleep. It appropriately distinguishes between activity-specific findings and the weaker total screen time associations. The paper discusses potential mechanisms (blue light, arousal, notifications) and notes small effect sizes for caregiver-reported outcomes. Recommendations appropriately target bedtime-specific behaviors.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.notes: This paper appears to use the same sample and similar methods as paper 033 (Helmer et al. 2024). They may be overlapping publications.
